entries.title: I — The Fortress Restored
entries.body:
Roderic and I have successfully restored the old fortress to its former glory. Time has not been kind to it, and whoever last inhabited this place must have abandoned it long ago. The main entrance was blocked by rubble and decay, forcing us to scale the thick ivy that clung to the walls. Once secured, we removed the ivy entirely to prevent others from gaining entry as we did.

In the basement, hidden beneath loose stones, we uncovered old scrolls, remnants from the days when the Circle of Golgari resided here. I once walked among them in my misguided youth, before the light found me. These scrolls hold secrets, dark knowledge to be sure, but things that might be turned into righteous purpose. We secured them in the library vault for now.

As for the Order, we are nearly ready. The fortress stands firm once more, and the Moot Hall is being prepared for the founding ceremony. Now we must find warriors willing to walk the righteous path and prove themselves worthy initiates.

entries.title: II — Initiates Gathered
entries.body:
Sidney Beauclair has done well. She gathered four promising warriors to join the Order as initiates. So far so good, but we are not yet ready to fulfil our true purpose. In two weeks, the Order's founding ceremony will be held, and with some effort, others will join our cause. Only then can the hunt for Osamiron, the dark sorcerer, begin in earnest.

I can't shake the feeling that the hooded acolyte Sidney encountered is tied to him somehow. Perhaps a disciple — like Mortazavi, or worse — a faithful Banorite under his control, like Othelen.

I told everyone that the tome 'Be Cruel to Conquer' was written by the acolyte. It was a necessary fiction. In truth, I must unlock the tome's secret, not for study, but to strengthen the Order's power.

Yes, some truths are better withheld. For the happiness of all mankind, I will stop at nothing to see justice done in this world once more. And may Othelen, blameless and martyred, rest in peace, knowing that his murderer will be brought to justice.

entries.title: III — Seeds Sown
entries.body:
With Roderic's blessing, I have sent word to my kinsman, Ceori Blackheart, the Captain of Summerhaven, proposing a Treaty of Friendship with his people and our own. The slums of the city, rife with shadowy dealings, may serve as fertile ground for knowledge. It may grant us an advantage in the battles to come.

Other seeds have been sown as well: the Lillywhite siblings. Their father, Steeve Lillywhite, was a knight of great renown before he disappeared. Sensing a mighty potential in them, I welcomed them into the fold without hesitation.

They are on a quest of sorts, a path of pilgrimage, seeking to awaken ancient ways of life, buried by time. Such pursuits often lead to hidden powers.

I am told that Amelia Lillywhite encountered a confused spirit beneath the ice island of Folda, which guided her to keys hidden in the deep, which lead to a long forgotten treasure. Though the relics proved of little worth to the Order, her keen instincts speak volumes. The apple, it seems, does not fall far from the tree. As for her brother, Allyn, his mettle remains unproven.

entries.title: IV — The Forbidden Tome
entries.body:
Yesterday, I summoned Initiate Ceori to question him about the forbidden tome he and Digi der Greif uncovered a week ago. Ceori claimed Digi held the tome, not he. Therefore, we were left without any clue it might have held.

I sent Ceori to Fibula Chapel, where the hooded acolyte was last seen according to Sidney Beauclair. He encountered Sentinel Myrwen Blackheart, stationed there to guard the graves. She was hostile at first, taking her job seriously, but yielded when he showed proof of his higher rank.

Ceori ordered her to assist him. After cutting down a nearby plant, claiming it might have been a spy in disguise, they followed the acolyte's trail to the latrine of the Fibula Bar. Remarkably, the acolyte had used the latrine as a hiding spot. Her journal was left behind, buried in filth.

The journal spoke of "seeds sown". Ceori believed they were dealing with a farmer. I believed otherwise.

It was obvious the journal pointed to the voodoo cult beneath the Vandura mountains. It was her home, and she must have returned there after having left Fibula. I dispatched Ceori alone to locate the acolyte.

Ceori faced death bravely, cutting through several cultists before finding the acolyte in a pitch-black corridor. Lacking any sense of subtlety, he announced himself as an Argent Gryphon, prompting the acolyte to raise her rod without hesitation. But, what the wizard lacked in wit, he made up for with sheer dumb luck. She died on the spot, but before he could get any information out of her.

He brought her body back to the fortress for examination. Her wrist was tattooed with the mark of Osamiron, the sorcerer behind the murders of Othelen and Zoldan. We also found a black rose in her bag, pressed in a piece of cloth. Somehow, I suspect it connects to the Red Rose of Fibula. Ceori, again, insisted she had been a farmer. May Banor grant me patience with this one.

entries.title: V — The Haunting of Fibula
entries.body:
I summoned the Initiates Adaelina Blackheart and Volgowrath to assist in the matter of Myrwen Blackheart, who had gone silent while stationed at the martyrs' graves in Fibula. They accepted without hesitation, but not before supplying themselves with potions and runes.

What they found was chaos. The chapel interior was in ruins. Stools shattered and fires raged, the scent of magic thick in the air. Myrwen stood amidst the wreckage, screaming for aid while holding a containment charm glowing with unstable power. The sorcerer Volgowrath reacted swiftly, uttering an enchantment to secure the charm, while Adaelina questioned Myrwen.

Myrwen claimed the ghosts of Othelen and Zoldan had risen. Othelen turned violent to make sure Zoldan could escape. Another sentry, Kjartan, had followed her. Myrwen managed to trap Othelen's spirit within the charm and pleaded that the others pursue Kjartan.

They tracked him to Scott's Inn in Senja, gravely wounded. He told them he battled Zoldan on Folda's ice where she nearly killed him. But she had let him live. Her mind, it seems, teetered on the edge of madness.

Following her trail into the caves, Adaelina and Volgowrath found themselves in a nightmare. Crazed frost trolls, wolves and minotaurs stirred within. Worse still, an ancient magic clouded their senses, and even Volgowrath's exiva was useless.

After what they felt was days of wandering, they found her. Zoldan's black spirit hid in a tomb of sealed coffins, screaming as if reliving past torment. She mistook them for her torturers, but when Adaelina mentioned Othelen, she calmed. Volgowrath then revealed the charm containing his spirit.

Zoldan agreed to rejoin him on the condition they cast the charm into the depths of the King's Gulf. They complied.

They returned this morning. I confess, I'm disappointed. The charm would have been valuable to the Order. But the threat to Fibula is ended. For now, at least, we have peace.

entries.title: VI — The Founding
entries.body:
Yesterday, our home rang with laughter and vows as we gathered Roses, Loremasters, the good people of Above and friends to witness the birth of our Order. Under the gaze of Banor and with the Red Rose's blessing, Roderic Rivershaw proclaimed the founding of the Argent Gryphon and acknowledged our official decree.

Rather than rule by decree, I decided to open for electing the Order's leadership. In a unanimous vote, I was chosen as the first Lady Warden, vowing to defend the realm with honour and faith.

Our four initiates who had shown their dedication to the Order were anointed in sacred rite:
– Digi der Greif
– Wizard Ceori
– Volgowrath
– Adaelina Blackheart

Each are now charged with completing the Griffin Shield quest to prove themselves worthy of the rank Shieldbearer.

The night ended with sacred vows and blessings. The Gryphon now flies, and with it, a new hope rises.

HAIL THE ARGENT GRYPHON!

entries.title: VII — Summerhaven Accord
entries.body:
I met with my kinsman Ceori Blackheart, Captain of Summerhaven, whose resolve to shield the common folk is beyond doubt. I offered an accord: the Order will see their people fed and clothed by the riches of the greedy exploiters. We will strike at the plantation lords!

In turn, Summerhaven's eyes and ears in the city will provide us with intelligence on the merchants, whose hands I suspect feed the dark cult that wages war upon us all.

Yet, our blades won't be enough. To move against the merchants risks the wrath of Thais and Venore. Without assurances from our benefactor, our plans cannot take root. Therefore, I must gain audience with Rye Ane, Legate of Red Rose, and secure Fibula's protection before our scheme is set in motion.

May Banor guide my tongue as well as my sword.

entries.title: VIII — The Red Rose's Assurance
entries.body:
It is done. The Legatus of Red Rose, Rye Ane, has given us the assurances we need to move forward. Should Thais and Venore retaliate against Summerhaven or the Argent Gryphon, Red Rose will protect us. The threat alone should be enough to deter them.

Now we must discover which traders support the voodoo cult before we raid the plantations. We could lure them out — make them reveal themselves, or infiltrate one of their homes and search for clues. Either way, I must wait until Warden Roderic returns from his travels. This is a decision not to be made lightly.

entries.title: IX — Roderic Sleeps
entries.body:
Roderic has returned, but without his usual vigour. He breathes, his body unmarked, but his mind is absent, bound by an unknown curse. He is beyond my healing powers. Months have passed, and still he does not wake. Only Barnabus' careful hands keep him alive.

Without my Warden, the Order is unsteady. I will not move against the voodoo cult while his fate remains uncertain. To act now would be reckless, and should Roderic be lost, the burden would rest with me alone.

We need knowledge beyond our reach. I must seek the guardians of lore. The ancient Loremasters may know such curses by name. There is also the Alanui Institute, which has gathered wisdom from many lands. The Arcanum Lyceum Conservatory may also hold artefacts that bend the rules of magic itself.

I may have to reach out to all of them.

entries.title: X — Alliances Sought
entries.body:
The Loremasters proved unwilling to align themselves with any outside order, refusing even to meet. The Arcanum Lyceum Conservatory, though promising at first, has already fallen to apathy — their offices now stand abandoned.

I have yet to approach the Alanui Institute, as I have been unable to locate a Director.

Not all efforts have failed, however. I have secured cooperation with two other factions. The first is the Circle of Stonehaze, an old order of Stonehome wisemen who have recently formed a centralised structure. We have signed a treaty of alliance, pledging aid in arms, alms, and sanctuary.

I have also made contact with a shadowed circle of mages known as the Arcanum, not to be confused with the Conservatory. I have pledged our knowledge and support in exchange for intelligence and destructive power, that we may uncover and destroy those who target us, and Warden Roderic in particular.

Once our agent Myrwen Blackheart has secured her place among them, I will move to formalise the alliance.

entries.title: XI — The Ringing
entries.body:
There has been no progress.

The Circle of Stonehaze and the mages of the Arcanum have failed to name the curse, let alone break it. Barnabus says all we can do for Roderic is make him comfortable. He is right, I see it in his eyes. But I can't give up. I will never give in!

Sleep escapes me. When it comes, it brings no rest. My thoughts are just noise, slipping through my fingers like sand. I remember the feeling. I have heard it before, long ago, in the war.

This is worse.

There is a sound beneath the castle. A faint ringing, low and constant, like a distant growl that never fades. I cannot silence it.

I cannot burden the Gryphons with this. I am their Warden. I must show confidence.

Yet I have never felt so alone.

I find myself thinking of Pertwee. If he were here, he would know what to say. Or at least, he would listen.

Something is wrong.

PS. Marlon der Nachtfall and Julian au Bellona have joined the Order.

entries.title: XII — The Demona Tomes
entries.body:
They speak softly around me now. Words hushed. They avoid my gaze when I enter, as if I wouldn't notice. I do not blame them. We all love Roderic, and they think he will die soon.

They are wrong, of course — he will not die. Not without my leave.

The healers have failed and are of no use to me now. Same with the scholars — their wisdom limited, worthless. I have wasted precious time on witless fools while Roderic slips further away.

Tonight I opened the Demona tomes. Volumes sealed for centuries, black with dust, filled with rites condemned by the church. They call such knowledge forbidden, but how can I afford to be selective?

Doneos was onto something concerning the mystery of life rings, though his research remains incomplete. I must know more. I care nothing for the judgement upon my character or soul, my people come first!

When the goal is righteous, sacrifice is required. This is my sacrifice.

entries.title: XIII — The Voice
entries.body:
I rose from bed to find the tower dark and quiet. Nothing but the familiar ringing in my ears. It has grown stronger. Or perhaps I have grown weaker.

I am as tired as ever. Worse.

Apparently I slept for days, though I cannot recall. I do not even remember closing my eyes.

There was a letter on my desk next to a blessed ankh. When I reached for it, the metal stung my hand. Strange. That has never happened before.

The letter was from Renai. He writes that Osamiron breached the castle and laid a curse upon it while I slept. It wasn't until I looked around the chamber that I felt it. Faint, but present. Death, lingering like a shadow.

How come I did not notice immediately?

And Renai, his words should stir me. Osamiron's name should ignite my soul to righteous fury. Instead, I feel... nothing. No anger. No indignation. No urgency.

Only the ringing.

I have written to Micu and Renai. They are to travel to the underground library of Drefia and gather whatever knowledge they can find. Books, scrolls, papers. Anything and everything.

Roderic, my friend — I will not fail you.

entries.title: XIV — Roderic Wakes
entries.body:
There has been no word from Micu and Renai.

The curse still clings to the mountain. I see it now in every corridor, the stone blackened like soot inside a chimneypot. The others do not speak of it. Maybe they do not see as I do?

The ringing is gone, replaced by a breathing whisper, echoing in my head. It is soft, gentle, as if sitting in the spaces between my thoughts. It tells me to submit, that I stop resisting.

Tonight, I did.

The voice led me below ground, down into the crypt where Roderic rests. The deeper I descended, the thicker the death haze became, soiling my lungs like smoke. I thought then to end it all. His suffering, perhaps... but more my own.

I wept as I walked, but there was comfort in the thought. Soon, it would all be over.

When I entered his chamber, I fell still.

Roderic's eyes were open and awake. His chest rose and fell with every breath.

He was pale, gaunt and frail, a shadow of his former self, but awake. His eyes met mine, and for the first time in months, I felt the weight upon me dissipate.

I fell to my knees in gratitude, but I did not seek Banor, nor the "righteous" Gods who abandoned us in our hour of need.

I prayed to the voice.

And somewhere in the darkness, it rumbled back to me contentedly.

entries.title: XV — REDACTED
entries.body:
Somewhere within the darkness, the voice rumbled and growled.

Then he revealed himself.

REDACTED

He did not stand before us physically, but as a presence in the mind. Vast, ominous, magnificent.

I knew from the look on his face that Roderic felt it too, and in that moment we understood: it was he who had answered our pleas where Banor failed, and now he desired something in return.

Our submission.

There was no hesitation. The words came upon us at once. Together we spoke as one.

"REDACTED"
"We turn to the southwest and kneel before you."
"We cast aside the false half-god and devote ourselves to your will, O Great One."
"Grant us strength, wisdom, and triumph over those who oppose us."
"We shall bind men to our will, as we bind ourselves to You devoutly."
"REDACTED"
"REDACTED"
"REDACTED"

The words burned in our throats as we uttered them, but the pain soon gave way to a gentle warmth, embracing me — stilling the long turmoil in my mind. At last, the noise faded.

I now understand what our purpose must become.

We are no mere brotherhood of honour. We shall bring order to chaos.

REDACTED

entries.title: Part One
entries.body:
Prologue
There are moments you remember not for what was done, but for the instant when you realize there is no going back. That day in Liberty Bay was such a moment.

And it began with a letter.



To the Lady Warden Valkera Blackheart,

I write to you in the hope of securing the services of the Argent Gryphon in a matter of grave urgency.

A band of Sabrehaven brigands, led by Raymond Striker, recently attempted a coup against my rightful rule. Though the uprising failed, they escaped capture and fled to the Liberty Bay Fortress.

They have entrenched themselves, demanding safe passage back to Meriana in exchange for ending the standoff peacefully.

Worse still, my daughter, the Lady Eleanore Silverhand, was taken captive during the fighting and is held within the fortress.

The situation grows more precarious by the day. The fortress’ food supplies are dwindling, and I fear bloodshed is inevitable the more desperate the brigands become.

Therefore, I call on aid from the Argent Gryphon to resolve this crisis.

Lord Percy Silverhand,
Governor of Liberty Bay.



Valarya flicked the letter across the table towards Valkera with contempt.

“The man is a complete lackwit. Why he has allowed the rebels free reign of Meriana is beyond reason.”

“Well,” Valkera began, remembering the last time she visited the island, “the water of that harbour is treacherous, isn't it? One wrong turn and your hull look like Venorean cheese. Can you imagine those round-bellied royal navy vessels go anywhere near that shore?”

“Oh, to be sure, but who in their right mind would attempt a direct assault? With a squadron of Gryphons, we could wipe them out by night. It would be ours before dawn.”

Valkera chuckled. Valarya was known for his silent, unassuming presence, waiting for others to tire themselves out arguing back and forth until they began searching for an alternative. That was when he would gently insert himself and sway the discussion in his favour. Yet among friends, he was like most men, prone to bravado and tall tales.

“Yeah, well… the Silverhands are jumped-up coin-nobles, right? Theirs is a low sort of cunning, more anxious than ambitious. Vain. Eccentric. You know... according to Ceori, Eleanore has a thing for these pirates. It would not surprise me if she went with them willingly.”

"Raymond... Raymond... hmm. It sounds familiar, I think I've heard his name before."

“Raymond Striker? Yeah, he-"

“Oh, wait!” Valarya broke in. “Isn't he the twit who got himself enthralled by some sea-witch?"

"Uh, yeah, that's him. And then some adventurer convinced the witch to lift the spell.”

Valarya cackled.

“Hah! What an idiot... can you imagine if she hadn't let him go?"

"It would have saved Percy a lot of headaches, that's for sure."

"So... what do you think? Shall we help Perkins?”

“Lord Percy,” Valkera corrected with amusement. “Yes, I think we must. We're still in debt to him. What is given can be taken away..."

“Summerhaven won’t like it. You might lose your cousin’s support going forward.”

He's right, Valkera thought. The Captain Ceori Blackheart has been a strong ally since the founding, and his connections in the slums of Liberty Bay were essential to leverage the Silverhands to begin with. She would have to convince him of the end goal.

“Say… do you see a way to please both Summerhaven and the Silverhands in this matter?” 

Valarya drowned the last of his flagon’s contents to allow himself some time to think.

“Well... if you settle a peace between Meriana and Liberty Bay—sure, but you're more likely to get Melody and Rye to hug it out, so good luck with that I guess."

“When the hells freeze over you mean...”

“Right? But if Raymond is half as dumb as Lord Porky... well, it mightn't be too difficult to hoodwink them both..."

Valarya’s eyes gleamed with intrigue, and he was dying for her to indulge him.

Valkera picked up the letter and ran a finger over the governor’s writing, letting it rest upon Eleanore’s name.

“Hm,” she mused softly, looking at Valarya with curiosity, “tell me how you would take Meriana in a night.”

And Valarya smiled.

entries.title: Part Two
entries.body:
Two days later, Valkera and Valarya arrived at the other side of the mountain, the only path leading to the fortress, where the governor and his men had set up camp. It was a pitiful sight. Half-starved levies armed with pitchforks and billhooks moved between tattered tents and camp followers. They were drenched in sweat from the humid heat and looked about ready to desert.

Yet among the miserable camp stood one lavish tent, and from it strode Lord Silverhand himself, clothed in velvet and fur despite the climate.

When Valkera and Valarya dismounted, Percy raised his chin rolled his eyes vanity. 

“Lady Warden”, he greeted awkwardly, offering Valkera a clammy hand. “Ah, and you must be the black knight.”

Valarya gave no answer except a curt nod.

Percy turned and gestured toward the fortress above them. 

“They have my daughter; the scoundrels took her!”

Valkera straightened her robes and clasped her hands in front of her.

“Lady Eleanore, Lord?”

“Yes! They seized her in the raid. My best men and I were protecting Trader’s Point and routed them easily enough, but Raymond snuck through Sugar Street and broke into Silverhand Manor.”

Valarya glanced up towards the fortress.

“We crossed paths on our way back,” Percy continued bitterly. “They refused to fight and ran! Now they hide behind the walls like dogs.” His lips curled in disgust. “They demand a ship and safe passage back to Meriana in exchange for my daughter.”

“You allowed them to escape,” Valarya remarked. Percy ignored him.

“I need this ended,” he continued. “The crown will have my hide if I lose a royal vessel to pirates. I want those brigands alive, in irons, and brought to Thais for justice!”

“Alive,” Valarya repeated flatly.

“Yes, alive,” Percy hissed.

Valkera stepped in between them, her eyes fixed on the fortress above.

“You want the Argent Gryphon to negotiate on behalf of Liberty Bay then?”

“The- uh…” Percy hesitated and cleared his throat. “The official position of King Tibianus III is that the Kingdom of Thais does not negotiate with pirates.”

Valarya let out a low growl and approached the governor, who seemed to recoil instinctively. Then Valarya reached out and plucked at the white silk sash around Percy’s waist that held up his fine leather breeches. The governor grabbed at them quickly before they slipped down.

“Let’s hoist the white flag then,” Valarya said, turning towards his horse.

Percy’s composure faltered, and he looked imploringly to Valkera.

“My daughter is in there,” he said quietly. “Whatever happens, Lady Warden, do not let her be harmed.”

Valkera studied him for a moment before finally nodding.

“You have my word, Lord Governor.”

entries.title: Part Three
entries.body:
“Turns out Thaian fashion isn’t wholly useless after all,” Valarya grinned. “Perkins’ sash makes for a pretty flag.”


The white silk shimmered beautifully in the sun and stood out clearly for the pirates upon the fortress walls to see.


Valkera sighed through a bemused expression.


They had raised the white flag upon a pole outside the range of the fortress walls. It did not take long before the gates creaked open and three figures emerged.


Valarya groaned as he saw the two rebel men walking beside Raymond, acting as his vanguard. They had strapped window shutters to their arms in place of shields, while Raymond’s hand rested upon the pommel of his sabre.


He stopped several paces from Valkera and Valarya.


“I see Percy sends mercenaries to do his killing now,” Raymond sneered. His eyes seemed drawn to their black cloaks.


“Lord Silverhand sends us to avoid the spilling of blood,” Valkera replied calmly. 


“Aye? His own, I wager. He spilled enough blood of the innocent to last a lifetime.”


“Your coup has failed, captain. Consider your options. The lives of your men depend on it.”


Raymond grinned.


“Yet here I stand while Percy pisses himself beneath the mountain.”


His men laughed.


“Your food supplies are dwindling,” Valkera continued. “The fortress stores little food this time of year.”


Raymond shrugged, but the grin on the faces of his men faded, confirming Valkera’s suspicion.


“We have enough hardtack to last us for months!”


“Without supplies, you are-“ Valkera began, but Raymond cut her off.


“Did you come here to talk about food, or do you have an offer concerning Lady Eleanore?”


Valkera clasped her hands in front of her.


“The traitor Eleanore shall share the fate of the rebels she serves. Like you and your men, she will be clapped in irons and sent to Thais to face justice.”


Raymond’s eyes narrowed as though searching for some trace of deceit upon Valkera’s face, but her expression betrayed only mild curiosity.


“Yes?”


“You will give us a ship. Supplies. Free passage to Meriana.”


“And if we don’t?”


“Then I will drag the governor’s daughter up the walls,” he snarled at her, “and let her dangle there with a noose around her neck!”


Valarya chuckled darkly and nodded toward Valkera.


“You were right.”


“What?” was all Raymond managed to say, his face flushing at the sudden turn in the negotiations.


“That you are utterly oblivious,” Valarya went on. “The governor knows about the bitch, so there is no need to pretend.”


Raymond took a step forward.


“Careful, black knight.”


“No,” Valkera interrupted him. “It is you who should be careful, captain.”


Silence settled between them before Valkera continued.


“You have the fortress, yes, but you are poorly equipped and poorly supplied. You possess no leverage, despite what you believe. The only road down this mountain is fortified by Lord Silverhand’s soldiers. Surrender now, and mercy may still be on the table.”


Raymond barked out a loud laugh.


“Mercy!? From Thais? You’re having a laugh.”


“Not from Thais,” Valkera corrected. “From us.”


His smirk faded slowly.


“And if I refuse?”


Valarya pointed toward the carcasses of dead cattle and horses beside the fortress walls, rotten black beneath the sun.


“You’ll end like that,” he said flatly. “A feast for scavengers.”


The men carrying window shutters shifted uneasily, but Raymond scoffed and turned to leave.


“You waste my time.”


Valarya turned to Valkera and nodded.


There was nothing for it then. Raymond had not believed her lie about Eleanore, and he intended to use her to leverage safe passage.


“Tell me, Raymond,” Valkera began haughtily, “do your men know the tale of the sea witch?”


He stopped dead in his tracks.


“The little fish-maid who had you wrapped around her finger. It is still sung in the taverns of Liberty Bay, you know.”


Raymond slowly turned back towards them.


“Watch your fucking tongue,” he growled.


“Oh, I was merely curious,” Valkera smiled innocently. “If a creature of near-human intelligence could enthral you so easily, perhaps it is no wonder Eleanore has done the same.”


Valarya snorted as Raymond’s jaw tightened.


“Yes,” Valarya added, “no wonder his little rebellion failed.”


The men behind Raymond exchanged sour glances as Valkera tilted her head slightly.


 “And had you been a real man, Raymond, perhaps you could have rallied more men to your cause.”


Raymond’s face turned from red to white, and he worked his jaw as he stared at her for a long moment.


Valkera smiled back.


“When next we meet,” Raymond said, each word measured with furious hatred, “I’ll cut off your hands and feet and feed them to my dogs as you watch, you arrogant cunt.”


He spat upon the ground before turning sharply away with his men.


The gates groaned shut behind them.


“How long do we wait,” Valkera asked.


Valarya smacked his lips and considered for a moment.


“The news should spread around the cookfires. If they don’t throw him out within a couple of hours, I say we opt for plan B.”

entries.title: Part Four
entries.body:
The governor had been furious. Broad-legged, he paced back and forth while listening to Valkera’s report, kicking at the gravel and gnashing his teeth whenever he heard of Raymond’s vulgarities. When she told him they would fetch a squadron of Gryphons from the Hideout, he resumed the same pacing as before. It continued for nearly an hour.

Finally, he objected and demanded that Valkera remain at the camp instead of leaving, insisting she take command of his levies and assault the fortress immediately.

It seemed Valarya had heard enough.

Calmly, he led the snorting and huffing governor by the arm far enough away that nobody could see or hear what passed between them.

After some time, Valarya returned with a pale, sweaty, and considerably calmer governor, who no longer had any objections to fetching reinforcements. Quite the opposite. He even offered them the loan of his Venorean hotbloods to hasten the journey, explaining that the horses were at good vigour during their stay at the camp.

Valkera guessed what had transpired between the two, but chose not to dwell upon the matter during their journey back to the Hideout. Yet later that night, when they made camp and Valarya smirked about sharp knives and fat hogs while butchering the evening meal, she smiled faintly to herself at the thought of the pale governor.

The war horn atop the tower signalled their arrival the following night, and Valkera assumed it was Mighty Micu faithfully keeping watch over the lands of the Argent Gryphon.

She and Valarya gently guided their borrowed horses up the steep mountain path. Every stone along the trail had been laid with deliberate care to create as much disorder as possible for an attacking force, but they knew precisely where the stones lay and avoided them with ease. The mountain wall along the path had also been fortified with positions for archers and sorcerers, allowing them to fire upon attackers before withdrawing further into the heights should it become necessary.

The Moot Hall of the Hideout clashed and rang as the Gryphons spilled through the door, some still fastening belts and chainmail after having been roused so abruptly by Sir Barnabus Runeholt in the middle of the night. The low murmur among the men and women died when Valkera rose from her seat and clasped her hands before her.

“Good. You will don no plate armour tonight,” she announced. “Take only what you need for climbing and close-quarter combat. No tabards. No torches. Sir Barnabus will hand out additional provisions. I want you upon the balcony within half an hour.”

The Lillywhite twins exchanged a glance until the sister cleared her throat.

“The balcony, ma’am?”

“Yes. Sir Valarya will explain further in half an hour.”

“Warden,” the hoarse voice of Volgowrath began, “the fortress… how many are we expecting?”

“No more than a score of poorly equipped but scrappy sailors—pirates, essentially.”

The room fell silent. Twenty pirates were no match for twelve Gryphons, but behind fortifications, that was a different matter entirely. The Liberty Bay Fortress was larger and far better positioned than the Hideout, and they knew well how few men it took to repel even a Thaian army upon the mountain slopes.

Valarya leaned against the wall behind Valkera with folded arms.

Aedelina Blackheart eventually spoke.

“What’s the plan, Warden?”

Valkera turned to Valarya who just smirked faintly.

“To let the heavens and hell bear witness to the audacity of the Argent Gryphon.”

A few nervous chuckles spread throughout the hall at the blasphemous remark.

“Have no fear,” Valkera added. “We do not send you heedlessly to Urgith’s corpse hall.”

Valkera motioned for the Gryphons to move out and nodded for Valarya to follow her. Together they withdrew to the Warden Chamber.

Then Valkera finally turned toward him.

“Are you sure about this?”

“No,” he admitted. “But I like our chances better than Raymond’s.”

Valkera studied him silently, trying and failing to detect even the slightest hint of concern.

“If he refuses the bait?”

“He won’t.”

“But what if he does?”

Valarya shrugged.

“Pray to Banor?”

Despite herself, Valkera smiled at that. Banor had turned away and was unlikely to ever favour her again.

Valarya fastened the grappling hooks over his shoulder.

“The important part is timing. Keep Raymond fighting long enough for his men to forget about the gate.”

Valkera nodded slowly.

“And once the horn sounds?”

Valarya’s expression darkened.

“No mercy—no pity.”

Silence lingered between them for a moment before Valkera nodded and extended her hand.

“Good hunting, Sir.”

Valarya clasped her forearm firmly.

“Break a leg, Warden.”

Then they parted ways.

Valarya disappeared down the stairs to meet the waiting Gryphons, grappling hooks slung over his shoulder, while Valkera remained at the Hideout for the night.

Her eyes searched the office for a special war horn, an item of deep sentimental value and a personal gift from Roderic when they founded the Argent Gryphon together. She usually kept it safely at home, but their audacious plan hinged upon more than just luck, and she needed all the favour she could get.

It rested upon a winter-wolf pelt atop her personal trunk of weapons and armour. As she picked it up, a thought came to her, and she opened the trunk.

Yes, she thought to herself, I think it is time to let loose the Dragontooth too.

entries.title: Part Five
entries.body:
Once more, the governor’s sash swayed in the wind, and it did not take long before Valkera spotted Raymond’s tricone hat between the crenelations.


Percy had protested earlier that day when she returned without neither Valarya nor reinforcements. She had assured him they had a plan, but he had turned deathly pale at the notion to assault the fortress with his levies. She told him to be ready and go at the sound of war horn.


In truth, their role in the plan was irrelevant, but Valkera preferred seeing the sweaty and hesitant governor craven before his own men. It could prove useful in the future.


Nevertheless, the time had come.


Valkera raised both hands in a gesture of peace and walked forward despite the reach of crossbows above. Everything rested upon Raymond’s temperament and desperation. Many stomachs were surely growling up there by now, and he could not be so foolish as to miss that his men may be at the edge of mutiny.


He needed to act.


And Valkera intended to give him that opportunity.


“Raymoooond!”


For a moment, nobody answered. There was just the wind blowing across the mountain. She moved closer.


“You again?” he barked out finally. “Have you come to beg for Lady Eleanore now?”


A burst of laughter rang throughout the fortress wall.


“No. I came because I finally understand why your uprising has failed.”


His expression darkened.


“Yeah? Why don’t you-“


“It’s because you are no true man, Raymond,” Valkera interrupted his retort. 


The laughter on the wall stopped, and Valkera continued.


“You are a man without honour and therefore no man at all. And you”, she turned toward the pirates upon the walls, “will never triumph with such a coward for a leader.”


Raymond stiffened and turned pale at the insult, unable to find words in return.


Valkera took another step toward the fortress.


“A weakling of a leader who cowers behind both stone and cloth, for that is what he does—hides beneath the skirt of the governor’s daughter rather than taking to battle himself.”


Several of the pirates exchanged sour looks, and Valkera understood that they had shared the same thought these past few days.


Raymond’s voice trembled with white-hot rage.


“Pray these walls keep us apart, otherwise-”


“Otherwise, what?” she interrupted again. “Do you truly mean to suggest you would dare fight your own battle? Don’t be ridiculous.”


“I have stood in more shield walls than you’ve drawn breaths, bitch!” he screamed so violently that froth gathered at the corners of his mouth.


“And yet there you stand, cowering behind cover. Were you standing behind those shield walls as well, pray tell?”


A murmur began to spread among the pirates. Valkera pressed the advantage.


“Hear me, loyal men of Meriana—you sons of brave settlers who defied the seas in pursuit of land and fortune. What would your forefathers say if they saw what pitiful wretch you have entrusted with their legacy? This miserable coward is so utterly unworthy that even the rats of Rum Alley would abandon him.”


Raymond slammed a fist against the battlement.


“Enough!”


“Then come down here,” Valkera replied, sweeping an arm invitingly. “Single combat. You and me.”


Silence followed.


Then Raymond laughed harshly.


“You think I won’t kill you?”


“Oh, I think you can’t kill me.”


Valkera slowly drew her sword—Dagor Dragontooth’s sword—The Justice Seeker.


“If the shit-eating pissant Raymond Striker kills me, he and his men may leave unharmed with provisions enough for the journey to Meriana.”


The pirates stirred at that.


“But should you die,” Valkera continued and pointed the tip of the blade towards Raymond, “your men surrender the fortress and shall be treated by the right that their ancestry deserves.”


Raymond seethed and stared at her as though trying to burn holes through her with his eyes. Behind him, the pirates whispered amongst themselves.


“Captain…” one finally muttered.


That was enough, and Raymond spat over the battlements.


“Wait there,” was all he said and disappeared.


Valkera lowered her blade slightly, and moments later, the gates groaned open.


And out came Raymond Striker.

entries.title: Part Six
entries.body:
Valkera instinctively took several steps backwards, sensing what might happen next. Now, her survival relied upon calm restraint and attention to detail.

 

And sure enough, Raymond came at her in a state of complete fury, sabre drawn low without uttering a word, intent only on a swift and decisive end to her insults.

 

It was precisely how she had hoped to face him too.

 

The sound of clashing steel echoed across the mountain as Raymond’s sabre crashed against The Justice Seeker, the legendary blade of Dagor Dragontooth from the wars. The sword held firm against the force, but the impact still sent a jolt of pain through her arm, and she staggered backwards.

 

The pirates on the fortress’ walls roared and cheered above them, goading their captain on. For Valkera, it was as dreadful as it was useful, for Raymond felt compelled to prove both strength and courage to his men. It also made him stupid.

 

He came at her relentlessly and without restraint. He was a large man with broad shoulders and powerful arms, shaped by years of pulling oars and delivering sword blows. He fought as Valkera had expected—brutally, with the intent to overpower rather than disarm.

 

Valkera, however, was not a woman raised with swords. She had learned late in life and was more frail than agile. There was no world in which she could overcome a man like Raymond in single combat. Attempting to tire him out was equally pointless, for his stamina would never fail before hers did.

 

So instead, she watched his face.

 

Every time he swung his sabre, his eyes revealed where he intended to strike—his grimaces betrayed the force behind the blow. Through training with Valarya, she had learned when to meet a strike with the edge of her blade to send it into the ground, and when to dance aside and let the sabre cut through the air.

 

Again and again, he struck at her with the sabre, and Valkera either stepped aside or deflected.

 

At one point she almost failed to evade him, and Raymond tore a great gash through her robes. She knew from the spectators above that the duel must appear terrifyingly uneven to anyone who had not studied under Valarya, whose upbringing had been nothing but the sword. Their jeers and cheers had grown louder with every swing of their captain’s sabre, and she noted how more of them crowded the walls to watch.

 

Raymond grinned savagely.

 

“Not so fucking cocky now eh, bitch.”

 

Valkera did not dignify the taunt with a reply. She circled around him, intentionally placing herself between Raymond and the fortress. She heard the spectators above grow louder, their voices more numerous than before.

 

Raymond lunged at her again with an intricate flurry of swings, and Valkera barely managed to stay outside the range of his blade, but she felt the pressure rising—her nerves screaming. One mistake and she would be dead.

 

Whenever she dared, she glanced over her shoulder, and each time she saw the same: more pirates that had left their posts to watch the duel. They leaned over the crenelations, excited to see Valkera's end inch closer with every sword stroke.

 

Then suddenly, Raymond’s sabre flashed toward her neck with such viciousness it would have taken her head off. Valkera stumbled backwards in panic, thrown completely off balance, and collapsed down onto one knee.

 

The pirates roared.

 

Raymond saw it and grinned.

 

Victory was his.

 

The bastard wanted to savour it triumphantly before his men.

 

His blade rose slowly in the sky, ready to be brought down.

 

This is it, Valkera thought, the farce is over.

 

She reached for Roderic’s war horn at her belt.

 

And for the first time since the duel began, Raymond’s grin turned into uncertainty, before twisting into gut-wrenching horror.

entries.title: Part Seven
entries.body:
Raymond’s sabre fell to the ground with a clang.

The pirates upon the walls could not believe their eyes. Speechless, they watched Raymond, who had been one sword stroke away from victory, become so paralysed with horror that he lost his grip upon the sabre.

They did not notice how death was gathering beneath them.

The dead animal carcasses along the walls suddenly began to twitch and convulse, and rotten flesh abruptly tore apart. Like weeds bursting between cobblestone, sword blades shot upwards in violent eruptions, and out crawled demon-like figures coated in decay, their chainmail drenched in blackened blood and slime. Fat corpse worms dropped from them and desperately wriggled back toward the filth.

Valarya’s plan had been cunning.

The pirates would never have ventured outside willingly, and only at the promise of certain victory would they have dared leave the fortress gates standing open. Distracted by the sight of their beloved captain defending his honour, the undisciplined pirates had forgotten their duty to guard the fortress against intruders.

While Valkera had slowly made her way back from the home of the Argent Gryphon to Percy’s camp, Valarya had meanwhile led his squadron through the mountains. Over rocky ridges and through hidden Cult caverns they made their way day and night.

Upon reaching the Liberty Bay Fortress, they had waited for darkness to fall before sneaking one by one all the way beneath the walls. With equal parts endurance and discipline, they had concealed themselves in and amongst the dead animal carcasses.

Now came the signal they had agreed upon.

The Gryphons stormed through the gates still left standing ajar.

Valkera did not need to see to understand what happened. With her eyes fixed upon Raymond’s despairing face and the death screams rising behind her, she realized the victory was total and utterly overwhelming.

She rose to her feet and walked toward Raymond, who could not tear his gaze away from the fortress.

He knew it was over.

His men were doomed, and they would not be spared.

Despite every setback, despite hope having abandoned them, his men had remained loyal to him until the very end.

Valkera realized then that they were not merely vagabonds and brigands.

They were his friends.

His family.

And now death danced with them inside the fortress while he stood alone outside the walls. She understood him perfectly. He had wanted to dance that final dance beside them.

She shared the same bonds with her Gryphons and with the Roses.

The light within Raymond finally went out, and he fell to his knees. He welcomed the killing blow, for then he would see his family again in the next life.

For a brief moment, Valkera wrestled with the urge to indulge him. He had earned it.

But the thought quickly passed.

No, she thought, Raymond still as value. His life still serves a purpose—my purpose.

She struck him hard across the temple with the pommel of Dragontooth's Justice Seeker, and Raymond collapsed unconscious, but alive, onto the ground like a sack of grain.

entries.title: Part Eight
entries.body:
The sight that greeted Valkera inside the fortress seemed taken from a fevered nightmare.

Those she assumed had first discovered Valarya and his squadron of Gryphons lay dead at the foot of the stairs leading to the walls, their weapons still undrawn. They must have rushed down to close the gates, only to be met by steel and fire before they reached the courtyard, for several of them had their feet and legs hacked apart entirely. Someone must have taken pity on them, for Valkera saw killing thrusts driven through their throats.

Tumult must have erupted up the walls then.

Several of the pirates had evidently fallen from the great height and shattered both legs and hips upon the hard stone below. One older, heavy man had unmistakable signs of a crushed pelvis. They too had been finished with thrusts through the throat.

The Gryphons had then divided themselves according to Valarya’s command. A handful had forced their way up the stairway past the mutilated pirates, while another group ascended the tower stairs to meet the defenders head-on. The burnt and frostbitten corpses along the crenelations told her of the work of Volgowrath and Myrenthil. They likely had not even had to take cover while bombarding the terrified and defenceless men atop the walls with fire and ice.

The last survivors had barricaded themselves at the highest floor of the main tower. Elara Blackthorn, Tyr Thundershield, and Lenorah Ironthorn had only just descended from there when Valkera entered the fortress. Their eyes betrayed shock at the brutality they had witnessed above. The pirates had surrendered, but the orders given to the Gryphons had been clear: all but one would die before this day was over.

Only four pirates remained alive.

They stood side by side in the middle of the courtyard, gagged and blindfolded for some reason, while Amelia Lillywhite and Ophelia Starwhisper guarded them. Apparently, they had not understood their orders.

Before Valkera could reach them, the cellar door opened, and out came Valarya and Renai Thunderstrike, gently leading the young Lady Silverhand by the hand. Behind them trailed Adaelina Blackheart.

When Lady Silverhand saw Valkera standing alive before her, a heart-rending shriek of grief and horror escaped her lips, and Valkera marvelled that such a young girl could produce a sound so bestial in nature.

Of course, she must have believed Raymond slain in the duel.

Eleanore collapsed on her knees and began rocking back and forth in anguish, repeating Raymond’s name again and again as though trying to summon his spirit back into the world.

Renai bent down and seemed about to place an arm around her shoulders, but faltered halfway through the gesture and instead looked awkwardly around for help.

Valkera quietly ordered Adaelina and Renai to carry the girl to her father, who ought to be making his way up the mountain by now. Should Lord Silverhand wish them to escort his daughter further, they were to obey.

Adaelina nodded dutifully and helped Renai lift the young woman to her feet, and together they disappeared out through the gates.

Valarya and Valkera met near Amelia, Ophelia, and their prisoners, clasping hands briefly.

Without speaking a word, Valkera looked questioningly at him and nodded toward the four surviving pirates.

Nothing more needed to be said.

“Would Initiate Lillywhite be so kind as to cut the throats of these two,” Valarya said, nodding toward two of the four men.

They breathed heavily in mortal anguish.

“Pardon, sir?” Amelia asked in surprise. Valkera suspected it was less confusion than her conscience wishing to confirm the command.

“Do it,” Valkera said.

Amelia obeyed immediately, drawing the sharp edge of her sword across their throats. They collapsed to the ground as life pumped from their necks. Afterwards, she gently pushed the tip of her blade into them to end their suffering.

Valarya turned his gaze on Ophelia.

“And you, Initiate Starwhisper, be so kind as to cut the throat of the tall one. Leave the other.”

They did not need to repeat the command.

The tall pirate collapsed too the moment her conjured icicle burst through the back of his neck.

Only one pirate remained alive, trembling, and his legs buckled beneath him in despair.

He kneeled against the ground with the blindfold tied over his eyes and a gag soaked through with tears and snot. His breathing came in ragged, panicked bursts.

Valarya crouched before him and untied the cloth from his eyes first.

The boy blinked wildly at the day light. His eyes were bloodshot from crying. He could not have been more than sixteen years old.

Then Valarya pulled the gag from his mouth.

The boy immediately began sobbing.

“P-please…”

Valarya seized him by the neck hard enough to silence him.

“What is your name?”

The boy struggled to answer through the shaking.

“G-Grenn…”

Valarya tightened his grip.

“Your full name.”

“G-Grenn Manacoil…”

The courtyard fell silent around them save for Grenn’s pitiful breathing. Amelia turned away in discomfort.

Valarya slowly looked up toward Valkera, who answered with the faintest nod.

Yes, she thought, this one too.

Valarya turned back toward the boy.

“Tell me,” he said coldly, nodding toward Valkera, “who is that woman?”

Grenn only stared blankly at her through tears.

“I… I don’t know…”

Valarya’s eyes narrowed.

“You do not know her name?”

Grenn shook his head desperately.

Then Valkera approached him slowly through the bloodstained courtyard.

The boy recoiled instinctively as her shadow fell over him.

“I am Lady Warden Valkera Blackheart,” she said calmly, “of the Order of the Argent Gryphon.”

Grenn quickly lowered his eyes.

Valarya suddenly grabbed him hard by the throat and forced his head back upward.

“Repeat it.”

Grenn choked in panic.

“L-Lady Warden Valkera Blackheart… o-of the Argent Gryphon…”

Valarya squeezed harder.

“Again.”

Faster and without stammer he repeated.

“Lady Warden Valkera Blackheart of the Argent Gryphon!”

Tears streamed down Grenn’s face.

“Good,” Valkera said softly.

Then she drew The Justice Seeker from its scabbard.

Grenn had no time to react as she struck him firmly in the face with the pommel of Dagor Dragontooth’s sword. One of his eyes burst apart instantly.

Grenn screamed.

Valarya leaned close and whispered into his ear while the boy writhed and sobbed upon the ground.

“You are going to crawl back to Meriana,” he murmured coldly. “And you are going to tell everyone what happened here. Do you hear me, boy?”

Grenn whimpered in agony.

“And if they ever think of assaulting Liberty Bay or the Silverhands again… you tell them who will come for them.”

Valarya tightened his grip around the boy’s neck.

“She will put every man, women and child to the sword while you watch, and then she will feed your entrails to the pigs.”

Valarya then wrenched Grenn to his feet and hurled him through the fortress gates.

entries.title: Part Nine
entries.body:
Percy’s levies made their way into the courtyard. They were visibly shaken by the sight before them, and Valkera understood why. Raymond’s men had been just like them, and it was more chance than fate that had placed them upon opposing sides. They had more in common than what divided them, and several turned tearfully away from the massacre.


Percy forced his way through the ranks of men. Apparently, he had made the journey up the mountain ahorse and looked well-rested, yet his face had turned pale at the scene.


“By the Gods… are they… are they all dead?” he stammered. Valkera was surprised at his genuinely horrified expression.


She sheathed The Justice Seeker and raised Roderic’s war horn to her lips. A low and mournful call echoed between the stone walls, long and lamenting. It was the signal for the Gryphons to assemble, and everyone lined up behind Valkera in silence.


Only Renai and Adaelina were absent.


“Blessings of the Gods upon you, Lord Silverhand,” Valkera greeted respectfully with lowered gaze and a curtsey. “The Argent Gryphon hereby returns this place carefully into your legitimate possession. The fortress is yours.”


Percy regained enough composure to bow as etiquette demanded. Then he spread his arms in disbelief, silently demanding an explanation.


Valkera assumed an expression of resignation and shrugged faintly.


“When Sir Valarya and I first met Raymond Striker, we warned him. Our orders were clear: the Kingdom of Thais does not negotiate with pirates. Should they refuse surrender, they would be denied mercy. This we have now accomplished, Lord.”


Percy looked helplessly around at the dead across the courtyard. He too noticed the unmistakable killing thrusts and drew the only conclusion.


“But you could have taken the survivors prisoner! They were to be brought to Thais and stand trial for their crimes!”


Valkera shook her head.


“No, Lord. With all due respect, we have a reputation to uphold. The Argent Gryphon keep their oaths. As promised, none would be spared should Raymond refuse our offer. You may now rejoice in the fact that Liberty Bay is most unlikely to suffer such ferocious pirate troubles again.”


“Your reputation?” Percy burst out. “You look like bloodthirsty murderers!”


“I object to the insult, Lord,” Valkera replied coldly before adding, “our will is that future enemies surrender the very moment we arrive upon the battlefield, without a single drop of blood needing to be spilled. For that purpose, displays like this are necessary.”


Percy ran a resigned hand through his hair and glanced around once more.


“And where in the hells is Raymond then? I don’t see his remains amongst the dead,” he muttered sourly at last, lips tightening with irritation.


“Unfortunately, he escaped. He must have fled during our assault and abandoned his loyal men to their fate. I imagine he shall find it difficult to recruit replacements now with such a tale to his name.”


Valkera allowed herself a faint smile.


“One more thing for you to rejoice in, Lord.”


Percy sighed deeply.


There was nothing more to say. His governorship and daughter were safe, and he had clearly hoped to prove his worth to the King by delivering captured brigands into the hands of justice. He would have to settle for this instead.


Turning toward his levies, he ordered the greater part of them to find a suitable place for a mass grave and begin digging, while the rest were to carry the bodies out before the rot made the labour worse.


When they reluctantly began their grim work, Percy turned back toward Valkera one final time and raised his chin.


“Your debt is repaid, Lady Blackheart,” he said tonelessly. “Return to your mountain. Farewell.”


He departed before Valkera could answer.


She motioned for Tyr and Lenorah to lead the squadron home, while Valarya and Elara were to meet her outside for a brief discussion. The captured Raymond was to be brought secretly to Ceori Blackheart’s Summerhaven, but not before he spent some time in the dungeons of Vandura. Just how long that would be, was up to him.


Epilogue

The lone surviver, Grenn Manacoil, never showed up to Meriana.


Half-blind, starving, and delirious from infection, Grenn instead found refuge with Meriana’s ally in the shadow of Liberty Bay: Ceori Blackheart. Neither his cousin Valkera nor Valarya had considered the possibility that Ceori’s Summerhaven had gone from merely sympathetic to directly active in the struggle for a free and independent Vandura.


And so, the tale of the Liberty Bay massacre spread like a wildfire in slum town.


Dead carcasses birthing demon-like knights drenched in blood. The freedom fighter Raymond Striker hexed by a witch in the mountains. The shadows of death she danced with long into the night.


With every retelling, the stories grew worse.


Soon the Argent Gryphon were not merely feared, but reviled and cursed as well. Sailors warned one another of the Gray Butchers in taverns and harbour, while Valkera Blackheart herself became known by another name: The Witch of Vandura.


For many months, Raymond was neither seen nor heard from again.


Then one night, long after most presumed him dead, he appeared without warning in Rum Alley. He bore the scars of imprisonment and wandered like a man whose senses had long since abandoned him. At times he failed to remember where he was, or even who he was. With the aid of Ceori, Raymond was quietly smuggled home to Meriana.


Whether Lady Eleanore ever learned that Raymond still lived remained unknown. Servants of Silverhand Manor whispered of the wailing maiden who rarely left her chambers. She was never seen in public again.


The Argent Gryphon received far fewer pleas for aid thereafter, as lords and merchants alike became hesitant to be associated with them. Yet when desperation calls, when all else failed and no other hope remained, it was the Argent Gryphon who were summoned at last.


And more often than not, the mere rumour of their coming was enough to quell all resistance.

name: The Heart of an artist monk

entries.title: The Heart of an artist monk, by Renai
entries.body:
The hands of an artist monk are bare. Bare and prepared.
Prepared for healing, for reading.
For opening a book and follow a sentence with soft fingers.

The fingers of a monk are magical and can create things. They can hurt, and they can heal. They can hold and they can drop. 

The monk heals itself and its friends. The monk kills to be stronger and wiser.
To absorb the knowledge of the world and grow a noble Heart.

So the Heart of a monk is also bare, because it is steady to absorb the shapes of things that receives with the tactful hands of an artist.

entries.title: Followers of the White Dragon, by Tekvorian
entries.body:
For truth and justice.
For order and prosperity.

One mind, one soul.
One kind, one goal.

Together through times of chaos.
Together through times of peace.

With wisdom and honor.
With strength and valor.

Devotion to our belief.
Devotion to our codex.

We are the Followers of the White Dragon.

entries.title: In Search of the Lizard Urn
entries.body: The Explorer's Society tasked me with finding the Lizard Urn in a village called Chor, east of Port Hope. I prepared my potions and runes, well-armed since this time I wouldn't be going with my friend Renay. When I arrived at the village, I found that the Lizards weren't very strong, but they more than made up for it in numbers?hundreds of them protecting their village. Since my intention wasn't to kill them, I used an invisibility ring to slip past them. However, I was betrayed by their best fighters, who didn't hesitate to attack. I was outnumbered and decided to flee, but without retreating. I pushed deeper into the village, always running until I found my back against a massive wall: the Kha'zeel Mountains, impossible to climb. The lizardmen kept searching for me and attacking.

entries.title: Through the Dragon Tunnels
entries.body:
Cornered, I followed the great wall that separated me from the Darama Desert until I found a passage that went into the mountains. Luck was on my side. Gathering all the courage I had and my remaining potions, I entered the unknown tunnels, hoping to find the desert and the city of Ankarahum.

My luck didn't last long, though, as I found myself in the most populated nest of green dragons, dozens of them, even with their young. I tried my luck fighting them, and thanks to being well-equipped, I managed to give them a good fight and take down several while exploring the caves, looking for the passage to the desert. Only one ascending staircase could be the way out, but then I realized the luck I thought I had a moment ago was gone, or perhaps I never had it and was destined to die in that dragon nest.

entries.title: The Red Dragon's Lair
entries.body: Before me stood a huge red dragon, a beast I had never fought before. With a single jet of flame, it dissolved my magic shield, and before I could even process what was happening, a swipe of its claw nearly sent me to the grave. I tumbled down the stairs, giving me just enough time to summon my shield again against the green dragons waiting for me there. I fought with all my might and cunning, and many dragons fell to my spells and runes, but a dozen dragons surrounding you is overwhelming. My shield paled with every blast of flame they threw at me; even the young ones attacked without mercy. All seemed lost. My combat techniques and strategies were completely overwhelmed. I could barely see the tunnel I came from through the green bodies of the dragons surrounding me.

entries.title: The Long Road Home
entries.body:
If only Renay were here, punching left and right, I thought. I could almost see him coming out of the tunnel and wiping out this green multitude, and then he would say to me, "Why didn't you use the temple scroll?"

That was it! The scroll. I took it, and with practically no life left, I activated it and appeared in Carlin. Oh, my dear Carlin! Still smoking, I drank potions until I was able to stand up again.

entries.title: Wild flowers, by Renai
entries.body:
Wild flowers popping up from snow
After a white mantle of thoughts

Wild powers from a Wrath god
That shapes a destiny of thorns 

Wild emotions of a monk 
Who seeks for pouring love in mould

Disordered facts in the path of growth
Enhanced by time and thinking

Displayed in the mind an engulfing flood
Binding both old and new streams 

Disciplined patience is his new law 
Embracing to his chest both flowers and snow.

entries.title: A humble druid's dream, by Micu
entries.body:
Soft breeze,
Autumn sun, 
The walls of Carlin in the distance,
A den of sleeping wolves and puppies running around with butterflies,
In the past remains the scourge of the fury forest, 
Only the simple passage of time remains without disturbances in the natural cycle,
And my body nourishing the soils.

M.M.
